name: Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop
name_original: Puerto de Sa Calobra — ida y vuelta desde Inca
description: Tackle the difficult 48.4-mile Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop, gaining 6367 feet through Mallorca's Tramuntana Mountains.
region_intro: Road cycling around Inca, Mallorca, benefits from its central location, providing access to varied terrain. The region is characterized by the dramatic Serra de Tramuntana mountain range to the west, offering challenging climbs and descents. To the east, flatter agricultural plains and rolling hills with vineyards provide less strenuous options. This diverse landscape supports a range of road cycling experiences, from demanding mountain routes to more moderate rides through rural areas. Best road cycling routes around Inca The most popular road cycling route is Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop, a 48.4 miles (77.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 37 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through the Tramuntana Mountains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Climb to Puig Major – Deià loop from Inca, a difficult 64.8 miles (104.3 km) path. This route includes a substantial ascent towards Puig Major, the island's highest point, offering extensive climbing. Local road cyclists also love the Coll de Sóller – Deià loop from Enllaç, a 70.1 miles (112.8 km) trail leading through challenging mountain terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 20 minutes. Road cycling around Inca is defined by the rugged peaks of the Serra de Tramuntana, extensive vineyards, and agricultural plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from difficult mountain ascents to moderate rides through rural landscapes. The routes in Inca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 6000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Inca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Inca?
A: Inca's central location provides access to diverse terrain. You'll find challenging climbs and descents in the dramatic Serra de Tramuntana mountain range to the west, while the east offers flatter agricultural plains and rolling hills with vineyards for less strenuous rides. This mix supports a wide range of road cycling experiences.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Inca?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes around Inca, catering to various fitness levels. These include everything from moderate rides through rural landscapes to difficult mountain ascents.

Q: Are there road cycling routes for beginners around Inca?
A: Yes, Inca offers several routes suitable for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. You can explore the flatter plains and rolling hills through the Binissalem wine country or towards Muro. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/4665067" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cami de Muro (Vineyard Route) – Orange Grove Road loop&lt;/a&gt; provide moderate rides through agricultural areas.

Q: Where can I find challenging mountain climbs near Inca?
A: For challenging mountain climbs, Inca is a gateway to the Serra de Tramuntana. You can tackle demanding routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1003237991/sa-calobra-coll-dels-reis-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33035262" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Puig Major – Deià loop from Inca&lt;/a&gt;, which includes a substantial ascent towards the island's highest point. Other notable climbs include Coll de sa Batalla, Coll de Femenia, and Coll de Sóller.

Q: What natural features can I see while cycling near Inca?
A: The region boasts stunning natural features. You'll encounter the rugged peaks and deep gorges of the Serra de Tramuntana. Keep an eye out for highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/384800" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ufanes Springs&lt;/a&gt;, a unique gushing natural spring, or the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4015385" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campanet Caves&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also offer views of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/77533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puig de Massanella&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical sites accessible by road bike from Inca?
A: Yes, several cultural and historical sites are accessible. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/203418" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lluc Sanctuary&lt;/a&gt;, a significant spiritual site within the Tramuntana Mountains, is a popular destination and offers amenities for cyclists. The Hermitage of Santa Magdalena, close to Inca, also provides panoramic views and is reachable by bike.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Inca?
A: April and May are particularly popular months for road cycling in Inca, offering pleasant weather conditions ideal for exploring the diverse routes. The spring months generally provide a good balance of comfortable temperatures and less extreme heat.

Q: Are there many circular road cycling routes available from Inca?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Inca are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33058952" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beautiful road – Camí Vell de Pollença loop from Inca&lt;/a&gt;, offering a moderate ride through scenic areas.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Inca?
A: The road cycling routes in Inca are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 100 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging mountain passes to picturesque vineyard routes, and the stunning views of the Tramuntana Mountains.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or bike services along the routes?
A: Inca itself serves as a comfortable base with urban services like bike shops and cafes that cater to cyclists. On longer routes, you'll find opportunities for refreshments in towns along the way, and the Lluc Monastery is a well-known rest stop offering food and accommodation.

Q: Is public transport available to access cycling routes around Inca?
A: Inca benefits from excellent public transport connections, which can facilitate access to various starting points for rides across the island. This makes it convenient to reach different areas if you prefer not to cycle directly from Inca for every route.

Q: Which routes offer the best scenic viewpoints?
A: Many routes in the Serra de Tramuntana offer breathtaking vistas. The climb to the Santa Magdalena Monastery, though short, provides panoramic views across the flatlands. Additionally, routes encompassing Coll de sa Batalla and Lluc, or those along the MA10, offer extensive views of the mountains and the sea. Don't miss the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/338110" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Puig s'Alcadena from the Mirador de l'Orengar&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: Finest asphalt, beautiful serpentines and little traffic. Most beautiful departure of the whole island.

This is a short and very popular climb in the area. It's less than two and a half kilometers long. It has an average gradient of 6.6% and some inclines of almost 9%. 
Be careful on sharp turns with tour buses, which often invade your lane while maneuvering.
Spacious hairpin curve, inviting for stop. great view on Sa Calobra road and mountains.
Arriving early ensures good seats. From 10/11 it will be unbearable with the buses. Otherwise fantastic

The little Ermita on the roadside does not have much to offer, but is certainly nice to look at. In addition, from here opens a first view of the Cala Tuent, the much quieter neighboring bay of Cala de Sa Calobra. If you still have fresh legs, you should definitely go down to the bay. Who once was there, knows why: Quiet and idyllic, the bay is the stark opposite to the tourist magnet next door. And if you're lucky, an old Mallorciner sells oranges on the beach.
A must do in Mallorca. If you were not there, you’ve missed the best of the island.

It is almost impossible not to stop at this popular viewpoint. The views of the bare rock massif that stands before the sea are incredible. It is not surprising that it is a classic among cyclists who visit the island.

faqs.question: Is the Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is widely considered one of Mallorca's toughest cycling ascents and is not recommended for beginners. It features a consistent, challenging gradient, with sections reaching 7-10%, and requires significant fitness and experience.

faqs.question: What is the best strategy for cycling this route given the 'descend to climb' nature?
faqs.answer: The unique challenge of this route is that you must first descend to Port de Sa Calobra before beginning the main climb back up to Coll dels Reis. This means you need to manage your energy carefully on the descent to conserve it for the demanding ascent. Many cyclists recommend an early start to avoid traffic and enjoy a more peaceful experience.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect on this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The route is entirely on a well-maintained, single-lane road within the stunning Tramuntana Mountains, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. You'll experience continuous, breathtaking views of limestone cliffs, the Balearic Sea, and the Mediterranean Ocean. The descent and ascent are characterized by approximately 26 hairpin bends, rock tunnels, and the famous 270° spiral bridge, the Nus de Sa Corbata (Tie Knot Bend).

faqs.question: What notable features and viewpoints will I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Beyond the engineering marvel of the road itself, you'll encounter iconic spots like the Nus de Sa Corbata (Tie Knot Bend), the challenging Sa Calobra Needle, and the picturesque Port of Sa Calobra. The Mirador de s'Entreforc offers panoramic vistas, and you'll pass over the famous Coll dels Reis and Coll de Sa Batalla from Caimari.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis route?
faqs.answer: The best times are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) when the weather is milder and more consistent for cycling. Summers can be very hot, and winters might bring cooler temperatures and a higher chance of rain or strong winds.

faqs.question: Are there specific times of day to avoid due to traffic?
faqs.answer: Yes, the road can become very busy with coaches, hire cars, and other cyclists, especially between 11:30 AM and 3:00 PM. An early start is highly recommended to enjoy a more peaceful experience and avoid the heaviest traffic.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop?
faqs.answer: While the loop itself starts and ends in different locations, many cyclists begin their ride from nearby towns like Inca or Caimari, where parking is available. From there, you'd cycle towards the Coll de Sa Batalla and then descend towards Sa Calobra. Parking is also available at Port de Sa Calobra, but remember you'll need to climb back out.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Serra de Tramuntana?
faqs.answer: No, there are generally no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Serra de Tramuntana, which is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. However, always respect local regulations, private property, and environmental guidelines.

faqs.question: Is the Sa Calobra - Coll dels Reis Cycling Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a challenging road bike ride like this is not practical or safe for the animal. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular adventure.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the El Camí IT4TR3 El Camí Illenc: de l'Alguer a Formentera, [CIMA IB09] Coll dels Reis - Sa Calobra, and the GR 222 - Ruta Artà-Lluc.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, there are cafes and restaurants available at Port of Sa Calobra. Additionally, towns like Caimari, Inca, and other villages in the Serra de Tramuntana offer various accommodation options and eateries for cyclists.
